text: The surgeon performs a sub-IMV dissection along the DJ flexure, with the camera positioned in the super pubic port looking upwards. Adhesions to the spleen are visible but do not appear cancerous. The tattoo in the retroperitoneum obscures the pancreas, complicating the medial splenic flexure release. The surgeon proceeds to take the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV).

The surgeon moves the camera to port two and continues the sub-IMV dissection for medial dissection. The ureter is visible, and the surgeon traces it to the IMA, identifying the ascending left colic branch coming off the IMA. The branch is isolated approximately two centimeters from the IMA, with the tattoo obscuring the pancreas from the inferior medial view.

The surgeon tucks a sponge into the surgical field and performs lateral dissection, taking a portion of the distal mesentery on the downside of the left colic branch. The dissection progresses into the medial plane towards the spinal collection, aiming to separate the splenic flexure from the spleen. The pancreas remains obscured from this angle, prompting a shift to a supramesocolic approach.

The surgeon separates the omentum from the colon at the planned proximal margin, entering the lesser sac. The omentum is carefully detached from the greater curvature of the stomach, revealing inflammatory adhesions that do not appear to be T4 invasion. Frozen sections sent for analysis are negative.

The surgeon attempts to create space to separate the splenic flexure from the spleen, struggling to visualize the pancreas from the current angle. Progress is made as the surgeon identifies the pancreas and works to detach the transverse colon mesentery from it, following along the base of the pancreas where it is fused.

The surgeon approaches from a lateral angle to separate the colon from the tail of the pancreas, ensuring the pancreas is safely out of the way. The lineocolic ligament is dissected, completing the mobilization of the specimen. The proximal margin is chosen, and a window is created on either side of the left branch of the middle colic artery, which is then divided. The proximal mesentery is taken up to the bowel wall, followed by the division of the distal mesentery on the downside of the left branch of the ascending left colic artery.

The surgeon positions the surgical field above the liver, revealing the renal vein and the tail of the pancreas. There is visible ischemia at the inferior pole of the spleen. The surgeon performs a side-to-side functional end-to-end anastomosis using a V-Loc suture, joining the posterior staple lines. Colotomies are made, and ICG is used to confirm perfusion of the inner lip.

The surgeon uses an inner 3-0 V-Loc suture to perform an anastomosis, starting in the middle and sewing outwards around the far side. The second inner layer is stitched back to the middle and sewn around the near side.

The surgeon ties two sutures together in the middle, completing a two-layer sewn anastomosis with a final outer layer of Tuo PDS. The dissection is reviewed to ensure no further ischemia on the spleen, with only a minor tip affected. The specimen is extracted through the suprapubic twelve-millimeter port, which is upsized to a Pfannenstiel incision.
